aliases: Steamed Chicken Prawn Dumpling with Caviar

english: Steamed Chicken Prawn Dumpling with Caviar

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for siew mai or siew mai with caviar. 'Dumpling food product' (FOODON_03302051) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FKP covers dim sum / steamed dumplings as a category. No specific siew mai with caviar code exists; closest_parent applied.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for siew mai or steamed dumpling with caviar. Ontology gap acknowledged.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Siew Mai with Caviar consists of 3 steamed dumplings, each approximately 30 g, totalling approximately 90 g.

nutrient_source.title: Siu Mai (Steamed Pork and Shrimp Dumpling) — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy; supplemented with HPB Singapore Foodo data for chicken-prawn siew mai and FAO/INFOODS SEA values for caviar topping estimation
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / Health Promotion Board Singapore / FAO-INFOODS
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact USDA or HPB entry exists for 'Siew Mai with Caviar' (chicken and tiger prawn variant topped with caviar). Values are adapted from: (1) HPB Singapore Foodo siew mai (chicken and prawn, steamed) as the primary base for macros; (2) USDA FoodData Central NDB 35188 'Siu Mai, steamed' for cross-validation; (3) caviar topping (~2–3 g per 3-piece serving) contributes negligible macro shift at per-100g level and is noted. Per-100g values reflect the composite steamed dumpling including wrapper, filling, and caviar garnish.

gi_source.title: Dimsum / steamed wheat-based dumplings GI estimation based on: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281–2283; and University of Sydney GI Database entry for wheat-based steamed dumplings (analogous food: wonton/dumpling, GI ~45–50)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for siew mai with caviar. GI of 48 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care) for steamed wheat-flour dumplings (wonton/dim sum category, GI range 45–52). The wheat wrapper dominates the glycemic response; the protein-rich chicken-prawn filling lowers the effective GI relative to plain wheat products. Caviar topping is negligible in glycemic terms. GL per serving = (48 × 14.5 × 90/100) / 100 = 6.3 g. gi_category is 'low' per ≤55 threshold.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~420 mg per 100 g; ~378 mg per 90 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit siew mai intake to 1–2 pieces per occasion and avoid high-sodium dipping sauces such as soy sauce, as a single 3-piece serving contributes approximately 378 mg sodium, representing about 16–19% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~48) due to protein-rich filling and steamed wheat wrapper
type2_diabetes.guidance: Siew mai with caviar has a low glycemic index of approximately 48 and a glycemic load of approximately 6.3 per 3-piece serving, making it a relatively suitable dim sum choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in moderation and without high-sugar dipping sauces.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat and elevated sodium; prawn and caviar contribute dietary cholesterol (~55 mg per 100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should be mindful of the combined sodium (~420 mg/100 g) and cholesterol (~55 mg/100 g) content from prawn and caviar; limiting to 3 pieces per sitting and pairing with low-sodium accompaniments is advisable to stay within heart-healthy dietary targets.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Siew Mai with Caviar is a premium Cantonese dim sum dish consisting of three open-topped steamed dumplings, each filled with a mixture of minced chicken and tiger prawn, encased in a thin wheat-flour wrapper and garnished with a small amount of caviar (fish roe) on top. The dish is a luxurious variation of the classic siew mai (烧卖), a staple of Cantonese yum cha culture widely enjoyed in Singapore, Hong Kong, and across the Chinese diaspora. The steaming method preserves moisture and produces a tender, juicy filling with a delicate, slightly springy wrapper. The caviar topping adds a subtle briny, oceanic flavour and a pop of texture that elevates the dish beyond the standard dim sum offering. Nutritionally, a 3-piece serving (approximately 90 g) provides around 133 kcal, 9.7 g protein, 4.7 g fat, and 13.1 g carbohydrate. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 48 (low), making it a relatively moderate-glycemic dim sum option. Sodium content is notable at approximately 378 mg per serving, which is a consideration for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish contains common allergens including wheat (gluten), crustaceans (prawn), fish (caviar), soy, and sesame. It is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those requiring halal certification without specific restaurant confirmation. In the Singapore food context, siew mai is commonly served as part of a dim sum spread during weekend brunch or yum cha sessions, and the caviar variant is typically found in upscale dim sum restaurants.

confidence_reason: No exact HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA entry exists for 'Siew Mai with Caviar' (chicken and tiger prawn variant with caviar topping). Nutrient values are adapted from the closest available approved sources: HPB Singapore Foodo siew mai (chicken-prawn, steamed) and USDA FoodData Central steamed dumpling data, cross-validated against FAO/INFOODS SEA values. The caviar topping contributes negligible macro change at per-100g level. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for steamed wheat-flour dumplings. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted estimates based on HPB Singapore Foodo siew mai (chicken-prawn, steamed) as primary base, cross-validated with USDA FoodData Central NDB 35188 and FAO/INFOODS SEA data. No exact record for this specific caviar-topped variant was found in any approved primary source. 2. Caviar topping (~2–3 g per 3-piece serving) contributes negligible macro shift at per-100g level; its primary nutritional contribution is a small increase in sodium and cholesterol. 3. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted source databases for this food category. 4. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted source databases for this food category. 5. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted source databases for this food category. 6. trans_fat_g is 0.0; confirmed negligible/trace in steamed chicken-prawn dumplings per USDA and HPB data. 7. GI of 48 is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care) for steamed wheat-flour dumplings; no direct GI measurement for siew mai with caviar exists in approved sources. 8. Halal status is unconfirmed for this specific restaurant product; caviar source and prawn handling would require restaurant-level certification verification. 9. Serving size of 90 g (3 × ~30 g pieces) is based on standard dim sum portion convention. 10. FoodOn match FOODON_03302051 and FoodEx2 code A0FKP are closest_parent matches; no exact ontology code exists for this specific dish.
